questions.question:
All engineering students should learn mechanics, mathematics and how to do computation.
        I                      II                                        III                                                          IV
Which of the above underlined parts of the sentence is not appropriate?

A. I
B. II
C. III
D. IV
questions.to_find: Which underlined part of the sentence is not appropriate

understand.plain: The sentence lists what engineering students should learn. The items listed after 'learn' should be parallel in grammatical form - they should all be nouns or noun phrases. Parts I (All), II (engineering students), and III (mechanics, mathematics) are fine, but Part IV 'how to do computation' breaks the parallel structure by introducing an infinitive clause instead of a simple noun.

keywords.explain: Grammatical parallelism means that items in a list must use the same grammatical form (all nouns, all infinitives, etc.).
keywords.example: She likes singing, dancing, and painting. (all gerunds - correct)

keywords.explain: In the context of sentence correction, 'not appropriate' means the underlined part disrupts the grammatical or stylistic flow of the sentence.
keywords.example: He likes apples, mangoes, and how to eat bananas. - 'how to eat bananas' is not appropriate; it should be 'bananas'.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence with four numbered underlined parts; Part IV breaks the parallel noun-list structure.

given.aim: Identify the underlined part that is not grammatically or stylistically appropriate

terms.meaning: Quantifier modifying 'engineering students' - grammatically correct
terms.example: All students must attend.
terms.connects: Serves as determiner; no issue here.

terms.meaning: Noun phrase acting as the subject of the sentence - correct
terms.example: Engineering students study hard.
terms.connects: Subject of the main clause; perfectly fine.

terms.term: Part III - should learn mechanics, mathematics
terms.meaning: Modal verb followed by a list of nouns - correct
terms.example: Students should learn physics, chemistry.
terms.connects: Establishes the pattern: nouns as direct objects of 'learn'.

terms.term: Part IV - how to do computation
terms.meaning: An infinitive/interrogative clause used where a simple noun is expected
terms.example: Should be 'computation' not 'how to do computation' to maintain parallelism.
terms.connects: Breaks the parallel noun list established by mechanics and mathematics.

given.plan: Check each underlined part against the grammatical requirements of the sentence - the list after 'learn' must consist of parallel noun forms. Identify which part violates this rule.

steps.title: Identify the list structure after 'learn'
steps.apply: The sentence says 'should learn mechanics, mathematics and how to do computation.' The items are: (1) mechanics, (2) mathematics, (3) how to do computation.
steps.note: Parts 1 and 2 are single nouns; Part 3 is an infinitive clause - this breaks parallelism.

steps.title: Test each option for appropriateness
steps.apply: Part I (All) - correct as a determiner. Part II (engineering students) - correct as the subject. Part III (mechanics, mathematics) - correct as parallel nouns. Part IV (how to do computation) - incorrect because it should simply be 'computation' to match the parallel noun structure.
steps.note: Replacing 'how to do computation' with 'computation' gives a grammatically and stylistically correct sentence.

solution.result: Part IV - 'how to do computation' - is not appropriate. It should be the simple noun 'computation' to maintain parallel structure with 'mechanics' and 'mathematics'.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; the question tests grammatical parallelism in a sentence.

questions.question:
The professor ordered to the students to go out of the class.
                              I                  II                    III                      IV
Which of the above underlined parts of the sentence is grammatically incorrect?

A. I
B. II
C. III
D. IV
questions.to_find: Which underlined part of the sentence is grammatically incorrect

understand.plain: The sentence contains a grammatical error in how the verb 'ordered' is used. The verb 'order' in the sense of commanding someone is a ditransitive verb that takes an object directly - it does not require the preposition 'to' between the verb and its object. 'Ordered to the students' should be 'ordered the students'.

keywords.explain: A verb that takes two objects (a direct and an indirect object) without needing a preposition before the indirect object in certain constructions.
keywords.example: She told the students to leave. (NOT 'told to the students to leave')

keywords.explain: An unnecessary preposition inserted between a verb and its object, making the sentence grammatically incorrect.
keywords.example: 'Discuss about the topic' is wrong; 'Discuss the topic' is correct.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence split into four underlined parts; Part II has a superfluous preposition 'to' after 'ordered'.

given.aim: Find the grammatically incorrect underlined part in the sentence

terms.meaning: Subject noun phrase - grammatically correct
terms.example: The professor announced the results.
terms.connects: No error; it is a well-formed subject.

terms.term: Part II - ordered to the students
terms.meaning: Verb with a superfluous preposition 'to' before the object 'the students'
terms.example: Correct form: 'ordered the students' - 'order' does not take a prepositional object here.
terms.connects: This is the grammatical error - 'to' is incorrect and must be removed.

terms.term: Part III - to go out of the class
terms.meaning: Infinitive phrase indicating what the students were told to do - grammatically correct
terms.example: He asked them to go outside.
terms.connects: The infinitive 'to go' is correct after 'ordered the students'.

terms.meaning: Prepositional phrase giving direction - grammatically correct
terms.example: She walked out of the room.
terms.connects: Correct prepositional usage.

given.plan: Examine the verb 'ordered' and its required construction. The verb 'order someone to do something' does not use 'to' between the verb and the person being ordered. Identify Part II as the error.

steps.title: Analyse the verb 'order' and its correct usage
steps.apply: The correct structure is: 'ordered the students to go out.' The preposition 'to' in 'ordered to the students' is superfluous and incorrect. Compare: 'He ordered (to) them to leave' - the 'to' in brackets is wrong.
steps.note: Verbs like order, ask, tell, command, instruct take a direct object immediately without any preposition.

steps.title: Verify each part systematically
steps.apply: Part I (The professor) - correct subject. Part II (ordered to the students) - INCORRECT: 'to' is superfluous. Part III (to go out of the class) - correct infinitive complement. Part IV (of the class) - correct prepositional phrase.
steps.note: Only Part II contains the grammatical error, making B the correct answer.

solution.result: Part II - 'ordered to the students' - is grammatically incorrect. The sentence should read 'The professor ordered the students to go out of the class.'

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; the question tests correct verb-object construction (no superfluous preposition after 'ordered').

questions.question:
If ' -> ' denotes increasing order of intensity, then the meaning of the words [talk -> shout -> scream] is analogous to [please -> __________ -> pander].

Which one of the given options is appropriate to fill the blank?

A. flatter
B. flutter
C. fritter
D. frizzle
questions.to_find: The word that fills the middle position in the intensity analogy [please -> ? -> pander]

understand.plain: This question uses a word-analogy based on intensity gradation. The sequence talk->shout->scream represents a rising scale of vocal expression. We need a word that fits between 'please' (mild, polite) and 'pander' (excessive, servile gratification) in terms of intensity. 'Flatter' means to compliment excessively but with some sincerity, sitting naturally between mild pleasing and the extreme of pandering.

keywords.explain: A word relationship where terms are arranged in order of increasing degree or strength of the same concept.
keywords.example: warm -> hot -> scorching; like -> love -> adore

keywords.explain: To gratify or indulge someone's desires in an excessive, servile way, often at the expense of principles.
keywords.example: The politician pandered to the crowd by making unrealistic promises.

keywords.explain: To praise or compliment someone, often more than is deserved, to gain favour.
keywords.example: She flattered her boss by complimenting his every decision.

y="143" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">mild</text><text x="140" y="123" text-anchor="middle" class="big ink">→</text><rect x="160" y="95" width="120" height="50" rx="8" class="hi"/><text x="220" ink">→</text><rect x="310" y="95" width="120" height="50" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="370" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">pander</text><text x="370" y="143" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">intense</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two intensity scales: talk->shout->scream and please->flatter->pander, showing parallel increasing intensity.

given.aim: Find the word that occupies the middle position in the analogy [please -> ? -> pander], parallel to [talk -> shout -> scream]

terms.meaning: To make someone happy or satisfied; mild, genuine effort to satisfy
terms.example: She tried to please her guests with a home-cooked meal.
terms.connects: Low-intensity starting point of the analogy, corresponds to 'talk'.

terms.meaning: To indulge or cater to someone's desires excessively and without principles; servile
terms.example: The news channel pandered to viewers by sensationalising stories.
terms.connects: High-intensity endpoint of the analogy, corresponds to 'scream'.

terms.meaning: To compliment or praise someone, often beyond what is deserved, in order to gain favour
terms.example: He flattered the committee to get approval for his project.
terms.connects: Mid-intensity word that bridges 'please' and 'pander', corresponds to 'shout'.

given.plan: Map the intensity scale: please (low) -> ? (mid) -> pander (high). Identify which option word fits the mid-intensity slot for the concept of 'gratifying or attempting to please someone'. Flutter, fritter, and frizzle are unrelated in meaning; flatter fits perfectly.

steps.title: Establish the intensity scale for the source analogy
steps.apply: talk (casual spoken communication) -> shout (raised voice, emphatic) -> scream (extreme vocal expression). The common concept is vocal communication with increasing force.
steps.note: The progression is along a single dimension: degree/intensity of the same basic action.

steps.title: Map the target analogy to the same intensity scale
steps.apply: please (mild: to satisfy or make happy) -> [?] -> pander (extreme: to cater servilely). The blank must be a mid-intensity word for the concept of 'trying to satisfy/please someone'. Flatter means to praise excessively to gain favour - stronger than simply pleasing but not as extreme as pandering.
steps.note: Flutter = to move lightly; fritter = to waste; frizzle = to cook/fry. None of these relate to the concept of pleasing/gratifying.

steps.title: Verify the answer
steps.apply: please (I genuinely try to make you happy) -> flatter (I compliment you beyond what you deserve to win favour) -> pander (I completely abandon my principles to gratify your every desire). This forms a coherent increasing-intensity sequence.

solution.result: A. flatter - it occupies the middle position between 'please' and 'pander' in the intensity scale of acts of gratification.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; the question uses word-analogy reasoning based on semantic intensity gradation.

questions.question:
In the given text, the blanks are numbered (i)-(iv). Select the best match for all the blanks.

Following a row (i) the shopkeeper, (ii) the price of a frying pan, the cook stood (iii) a row to withdraw cash (iv) the ATM booth.

A. (i) with  (ii) over  (iii) at  (iv) with
B. (i) at    (ii) over  (iii) over (iv) in
C. (i) with  (ii) over  (iii) in  (iv) at
D. (i) over  (ii) with  (iii) over (iv) at
questions.to_find: The correct set of prepositions to fill all four blanks in the passage

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of fixed prepositional collocations in English. Each blank requires a specific preposition that idiomatically combines with the noun or verb it accompanies. 'Row with someone' (quarrel), 'over a price' (about/concerning), 'stand in a row/queue', and 'at the ATM booth' are all standard English idioms.

keywords.explain: A pair or group of words that habitually appear together in natural language use; changing one word makes it sound unnatural.
keywords.example: 'Make a decision' is a collocation; 'do a decision' is not natural.

keywords.explain: A fixed expression where a specific preposition must follow a verb, noun, or adjective; the choice cannot always be predicted by logic alone.
keywords.example: 'Interested in', 'responsible for', 'row with someone' are prepositional idioms.

understand.visual_alt: A passage with four numbered blanks requiring the correct prepositions based on English collocations.

given.aim: Select the correct prepositions for all four blanks by matching idiomatic English usage

terms.meaning: 'Row with someone' = to quarrel/argue with that person; 'with' is the correct preposition after 'row' when indicating the person argued with
terms.example: I had a row with my colleague over the project deadline.
terms.connects: Establishes the cause: a quarrel with the shopkeeper.

terms.meaning: 'Over the price' = concerning or about the price; 'over' indicates the subject of dispute
terms.example: They argued over the price of the car.
terms.connects: Specifies what the row was about.

terms.meaning: 'Stand in a row/queue' = to wait in a line; 'in' is the correct preposition
terms.example: People stood in a row waiting for tickets.
terms.connects: Describes the cook joining a queue at the ATM.

terms.meaning: 'At the ATM booth' = at a specific location/point; 'at' is used for specific locations or points
terms.example: He withdrew money at the ATM.
terms.connects: Indicates the location where the cash is being withdrawn.

given.plan: Analyse each blank using standard prepositional collocations: 'row with' (quarrel with), 'over' (about/concerning), 'in a row/queue' (standing in line), and 'at' (a specific location). Match to option C.

steps.title: Determine blank (i): ___ the shopkeeper
steps.apply: The idiom 'have a row with someone' uses 'with' to indicate the person one is quarrelling with. So (i) = 'with'.
steps.note: Options B ('at') and D ('over') are wrong here; 'row at' and 'row over' do not convey the argument with the shopkeeper.

steps.title: Determine blank (ii): ___ the price
steps.apply: 'Over' indicates the subject matter of the argument. 'Row over the price' = argue about the price. So (ii) = 'over'.
steps.note: Option D uses 'with' for blank ii, which would incorrectly read 'with the price' - not idiomatic.

steps.title: Determine blank (iii): stood ___ a row
steps.apply: 'In a row' or 'in a queue' means standing in a line. So (iii) = 'in'.
steps.note: Options A ('at') and B ('over') are incorrect; 'stood at a row' and 'stood over a row' do not mean queuing.

steps.title: Determine blank (iv): cash ___ the ATM booth
steps.apply: 'At' is used with specific points or locations like ATM booths, bus stops, shops. So (iv) = 'at'.
steps.note: This matches option C: (i) with, (ii) over, (iii) in, (iv) at.

solution.result: C. (i) with, (ii) over, (iii) in, (iv) at - each blank is filled with the correct prepositional collocation.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; the question tests idiomatic prepositional collocations in English.

questions.question:
Inhaling the smoke from a burning ____________ could ____________ you quickly.

A. tire / tier
B. tire / tyre
C. tyre / tire
D. tyre / tier
questions.to_find: The correct pair of homophones to fill both blanks in the sentence

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of homophones and near-homophones. 'Tyre' (British English spelling) is the rubber covering of a wheel - burning tyres produce toxic smoke. 'Tire' (American/universal English spelling) means to exhaust or fatigue someone. The sentence makes sense as: 'Inhaling smoke from a burning tyre could tire you quickly.' The other words - tier (a level/row) and the wrong spelling in the wrong place - do not fit.

keywords.explain: Words that sound alike (or nearly alike) but have different spellings and meanings.
British English spelling for the rubber ring that fits around a wheel of a vehicle.
keywords.example: The car's front tyre was punctured.

keywords.explain: To become weary or to make someone weary/exhausted; also the American English spelling of tyre.
keywords.example: Running a marathon will tire even the fittest athletes.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence with two blanks requiring tyre (rubber wheel covering, noun) and tire (to exhaust, verb).

given.aim: Select the correct word pair where the first blank is a noun (something that burns) and the second is a verb (to exhaust/fatigue)

terms.meaning: Something that can be physically burned and produce harmful smoke; must be a noun referring to a burnable object
terms.example: Burning tyres produce thick black toxic smoke - a well-known environmental hazard.
terms.connects: The word needed is 'tyre' - the rubber vehicle tyre.

terms.meaning: A verb meaning to exhaust, weaken, or fatigue someone rapidly
terms.example: The toxic fumes from burning rubber could tire you (exhaust you) very quickly.
terms.connects: The word needed is 'tire' - to make weary/exhausted.

terms.meaning: A row, level, or layer in a series (e.g., a tiered cake)
terms.example: The stadium has three tiers of seating.
terms.connects: Cannot burn and cannot mean to exhaust - ruled out for both blanks.

given.plan: First blank: identify a noun that can be burned and produces harmful smoke -> 'tyre'. Second blank: identify a verb meaning to exhaust -> 'tire'. Confirm option C: tyre / tire.

steps.title: Analyse the first blank: 'a burning ___'
steps.apply: The word must be a noun that can be 'burning' (physically on fire). Options: tire (American spelling of tyre - physical object, can burn), tyre (British spelling of the rubber wheel covering - can burn), tier (a level/layer - not typically burned for smoke).
steps.note: Both 'tire' (American) and 'tyre' (British) refer to the rubber ring, but the sentence distinguishes them as two different words. In this question, 'tyre' (blank 1) and 'tire' (blank 2) are treated as distinct with different roles.

steps.title: Analyse the second blank: 'could ___ you quickly'
steps.apply: The word must be a verb meaning to exhaust or weaken. 'Tire' works perfectly: 'could tire you' = could exhaust you. 'Tyre' is a noun and cannot be used as a verb meaning to fatigue. 'Tier' also cannot function as a verb meaning to exhaust.

steps.title: Match to the correct option
steps.apply: First blank = tyre, second blank = tire. This matches Option C: tyre / tire. Full sentence: 'Inhaling the smoke from a burning tyre could tire you quickly.'
steps.note: Options A (tire/tier) and D (tyre/tier) use 'tier' as the verb which is wrong. Option B (tire/tyre) swaps the roles incorrectly.

solution.result: C. tyre / tire - 'Inhaling the smoke from a burning tyre could tire you quickly.' Tyre = rubber wheel covering; tire = to exhaust.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; the question tests correct usage of homophones tyre (noun) and tire (verb) in context.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence:

His rather casual remarks on politics ________ his lack of seriousness about the subject.

A. masked
B. belied
C. betrayed
D. suppressed
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word to complete the sentence about casual remarks revealing a lack of seriousness

understand.plain: The sentence says that someone's casual remarks about politics revealed or exposed their lack of seriousness on the subject. The blank needs a word that means 'to reveal or expose something unintentionally'. 'Betrayed' fits perfectly - in this context it means 'revealed or disclosed unintentionally', as in 'his tone betrayed his nervousness'. The other options (masked = hid, belied = contradicted, suppressed = held back) do not match this meaning.

keywords.explain: In this usage, 'betray' means to reveal something unintentionally or to be evidence of something - not the sense of disloyalty.
keywords.example: His trembling hands betrayed his anxiety. / Her smile betrayed her relief.

keywords.explain: To give a false impression of something; to contradict or misrepresent.
keywords.example: Her cheerful expression belied her inner sadness. (Her expression contradicted her sadness - made her seem happy when she was not)
She masked her disappointment with a smile.

understand.visual_alt: A word-choice sentence where 'betrayed' means 'revealed unintentionally' - the casual remarks exposed his lack of seriousness.

given.aim: Select the word that best conveys that his casual remarks revealed/exposed his lack of seriousness

terms.meaning: To conceal or hide something - opposite of what the sentence needs
terms.example: His confident tone masked his fear.
terms.connects: If the remarks 'masked' his lack of seriousness, they would be hiding it - but the context implies revelation, not concealment.

terms.meaning: To give a false impression that contradicts reality
terms.example: Her enthusiastic response belied her true disinterest.
terms.connects: If the remarks 'belied' his lack of seriousness, they would appear to contradict it (make him seem serious) - not the intended meaning.

terms.meaning: To reveal or expose unintentionally; to be evidence of (a feeling, quality, or state)
terms.example: His fumbling betrayed his nervousness.
terms.connects: The casual remarks unintentionally revealed/exposed his lack of seriousness - this matches 'betrayed' perfectly.

terms.meaning: To hold back, restrain, or prevent something from being expressed
terms.example: She suppressed a laugh.
terms.connects: If remarks 'suppressed' his lack of seriousness, they would be holding it back - opposite of revealing it.

given.plan: The sentence structure is: [cause] ___ [effect being revealed]. The blank must mean 'reveal/expose unintentionally'. Only 'betrayed' carries this precise meaning; mask and suppress mean concealment, while belie means to contradict/misrepresent.

steps.title: Determine the logical relationship in the sentence
steps.apply: The sentence implies that the casual remarks served as evidence of or revealed his lack of seriousness. The blank must mean 'to reveal, show, or expose' something - specifically an unintentional disclosure.
steps.note: The adverb 'rather' emphasises the casualness, reinforcing that the remarks unintentionally showed his attitude.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options by meaning
steps.apply: A. masked = hid (opposite of revealed - WRONG). B. belied = contradicted or gave false impression of (would mean the remarks made him appear serious when he was not - WRONG meaning). D. suppressed = held back (suggests concealment - WRONG). C. betrayed = revealed unintentionally (CORRECT).
steps.note: 'Betrayed' in this non-loyalty sense means to be inadvertent evidence of something, which exactly fits the sentence.

solution.result: C. betrayed - 'His rather casual remarks on politics betrayed his lack of seriousness about the subject.' Here 'betrayed' means 'revealed unintentionally'.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula applies; the question tests vocabulary precision - specifically the sense of 'betray' meaning to reveal unintentionally.
